---
title: "Week 9: Temperature"
subtitle: "Urban Heat, Social Inequality, and Land Surface Temperature Analysis over Beijing in GEE and R"
date: "2026-03-21"
bibliography: references.bib
---

## 1. Content Summary

### 1.1 Urban Heat as a Social Problem, Not Just a Physical One

This week reframed **Land Surface Temperature (LST)** as something more than a geophysical measurement — it is a spatial record of accumulated planning decisions. [@klinenberg1999]'s account of the 1995 Chicago heatwave is the anchor here: heat mortality was not random but concentrated in **low-income, socially isolated neighbourhoods** where poor housing stock, absent green canopy, and lack of air conditioning compounded the physical heat. The disaster was social before it was meteorological.

[@wilson2020] connects this directly to **redlining** — the 20th-century US practice of systematically denying mortgage investment to minority neighbourhoods. Those boundaries have left a measurable **thermal legacy**: redlined areas today tend to have more **impervious surface**, less tree cover, and higher summer temperatures than areas that received investment. [@nowak2022] quantifies this gap through tree cover and ecosystem service values, showing consistent disparities across redlining grades in US cities. The implication is that mapping urban heat without mapping **urban inequality** misses half the picture.

### 1.2 Two Sensors, Two Trade-offs: Landsat and MODIS

LST can be retrieved from both **Landsat 8** and **MODIS**, but they offer fundamentally different trade-offs between **spatial and temporal resolution**:

* **Landsat ST_B10** — 30m spatial resolution, ~16-day revisit, scale factor: $T(K) = DN \times 0.00341802 + 149.0$, then subtract 273.1 for Celsius. Good for mapping **spatial patterns** within a city.
* **MODIS (Aqua MYD11A1 + Terra MOD11A1)** — 1km resolution, ~1-2 day revisit, scale factor: $\times 0.02 - 273.1$. Unsuitable for within-city spatial detail but excellent for **time series** analysis.

| Sensor | Resolution | Revisit | Images (May–Sep 2022) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Landsat 8 | 30m | ~16 days | 13 | Spatial patterns |
| MODIS Aqua | 1km | ~1 day | 153 | Time series |
| MODIS Terra | 1km | ~1 day | 153 | Time series |

: Sensor comparison for LST analysis {#tbl-sensors}

[@maclachlan2021] demonstrate how combining these two sources — Landsat for **spatial specificity** and MODIS for **temporal density** — can support evidence-based urban heat mitigation planning.

### 1.3 The Heat Index: From Raw LST to Policy-Relevant Classification

Raw LST values are hard for planners to act on directly. The workshop introduced a **percentile-based heat index** modelled on BBC methodology, which classifies each administrative unit into one of five heat hazard levels based on where its mean summer temperature falls in the distribution:

$$\text{Heat Level} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } T \leq p_{40} \\ 2 & \text{if } p_{40} < T \leq p_{70} \\ 3 & \text{if } p_{70} < T \leq p_{90} \\ 4 & \text{if } p_{90} < T \leq p_{99} \\ 5 & \text{if } T > p_{99} \end{cases}$$

This converts a continuous temperature surface into a **discrete risk ranking** that can guide resource allocation — directing cooling centres, tree-planting initiatives, or emergency health services to **Level 4 and 5** areas [@gee_book2023].

::: {.callout-important}
## Percentile Rank vs. Absolute Temperature
Classifying by **percentile rank** rather than fixed temperature thresholds means the index is **relative** to the local distribution, not absolute. A Level 5 district in Beijing is the hottest relative to other districts in the municipality, but not necessarily hotter than a Level 3 area in a different city. This is appropriate for **within-city targeting** but not for cross-city comparison.
:::

---

## 2. Applications of the Content

### 2.1 Study Area: Beijing Municipality

I chose **Beijing** as the study area for this week, following the workshop structure exactly. Beijing municipality is an ideal case for urban heat analysis: it is a large, dense megacity surrounded by mountainous rural areas, meaning the contrast between **hot urban core** and **cooler periphery** is sharp and spatially interpretable. The municipality encompasses both the dense commercial and residential centre and extensive outer districts including mountainous terrain to the north and west.

For administrative boundaries I used **GADM 4.1** rather than FAO GAUL, because GAUL Level 2 returns only 2 features for Beijing — insufficient for meaningful per-district analysis. GADM Level 2 provides the overall municipality boundary (used for `filterBounds` and `clip`), while GADM Level 3 provides **11 spatial units**: 10 outer districts plus one combined central urban area encompassing the six inner districts that GADM aggregates into a single feature.

::: {.callout-note}
## GAUL vs GADM for Chinese Cities
FAO/GAUL/2015/level2 filtered to `ADM1_CODE == 899` returns only 2 features for Beijing — the dataset does not subdivide Chinese municipalities at the district level. GADM 4.1 Level 3, uploaded as a GEE Asset, provides **11 meaningful spatial units** and was used for all per-district analysis. The GADM Level 2 union was used for the overall Beijing boundary to ensure spatial consistency.
:::

### 2.2 Workshop: Loading and Styling the Study Area Boundary

The first step follows the workshop exactly: load the global Level 1 administrative dataset for background reference, then define the study area boundary. Here the Beijing boundary is derived from GADM Level 2 using `.union()` to merge all district polygons into a single municipality outline, and the 11 GADM Level 3 districts are loaded separately for per-unit analysis.

<details>
<summary>Show GEE code</summary>

```javascript
//--------------------------Vector data---------------------------

var dataset = ee.FeatureCollection("FAO/GAUL/2015/level1");

var dataset_style = dataset.style({
  color: '1e90ff',
  width: 2,
  fillColor: '00000000',
});

Map.addLayer(dataset, {}, 'Second Level Administrative Units_1');

// Use GADM Level 2 for Beijing boundary — consistent with district-level data below
var gadm2 = ee.FeatureCollection('projects/week6-gee-coursework/assets/gadm41_CHN_2');
var Beijing = gadm2.filter(ee.Filter.eq('NAME_1', 'Beijing')).union();

Map.addLayer(Beijing, {}, 'Beijing');
```

</details>

### 2.3 Workshop: Landsat 8 Land Surface Temperature

The Landsat workflow follows the workshop exactly. The thermal band **ST_B10** records top-of-atmosphere brightness temperature in **Kelvin**; after applying the standard scale factor and subtracting 273.1, the result is surface temperature in **Celsius**. A cloud cover threshold of **< 1%** is used — Beijing's relatively dry summer climate makes this feasible, and the resulting 13 scenes provide adequate coverage.

<details>
<summary>Show GEE code</summary>

```javascript
//--------------------------Landsat data---------------------------

function applyScaleFactors(image) {
  var opticalBands = image.select('SR_B.').multiply(0.0000275).add(-0.2);
  var thermalBands = image.select('ST_B.*').multiply(0.00341802).add(149.0);
  return image.addBands(opticalBands, null, true)
              .addBands(thermalBands, null, true);
}

var landsat = ee.ImageCollection('LANDSAT/LC08/C02/T1_L2')
  .filterDate('2022-01-01', '2022-10-10')
  .filter(ee.Filter.calendarRange(5, 9,'month'))
  .filterBounds(Beijing)
  .filter(ee.Filter.lt("CLOUD_COVER", 1))
  .map(applyScaleFactors);

print(landsat);

var subtracted = landsat.select('ST_B10').map(function (image) {
  var subtract = image.subtract(273.1);
  var mask = subtract.gt(0);
  var mask_0 = subtract.updateMask(mask);
  return mask_0;
});

var subtracted_mean = subtracted.reduce(ee.Reducer.mean())
  .clip(Beijing);

var vis_params = {
  min: 20,
  max: 55,
  palette: [
    '040274', '040281', '0502a3', '0502b8', '0502ce', '0502e6',
    '0602ff', '235cb1', '307ef3', '269db1', '30c8e2', '32d3ef',
    '3be285', '3ff38f', '86e26f', '3ae237', 'b5e22e', 'd6e21f',
    'fff705', 'ffd611', 'ffb613', 'ff8b13', 'ff6e08', 'ff500d',
    'ff0000', 'de0101', 'c21301', 'a71001', '911003'
  ]
};

Map.addLayer(subtracted_mean, vis_params, 'Landsat Temp');
```

</details>

![Figure 1: Mean summer Land Surface Temperature (May–September 2022) derived from Landsat 8 ST_B10 over Beijing municipality. Higher temperatures (yellow–green) concentrate in the southern plains and the central urban core, while the mountainous northern and western districts (Huairou, Yanqing, Mentougou) remain markedly cooler (deep blue). Note that while the palette extends to red (max = 55°C), the maximum observed mean LST in Beijing stays within the green–yellow range (~35–38°C), confirming that extreme pixel-level temperatures are averaged out across the summer period.](images/week9_landsat_lst.png)

### 2.4 Workshop: MODIS Time Series and Per-District Analysis

With only 13 Landsat scenes available, **temporal analysis** is limited. MODIS compensates — merging Aqua (153 images) and Terra (153 images) gives **306 observations** for the same period, enabling a meaningful temperature time series that reveals seasonal dynamics invisible to Landsat alone.

<details>
<summary>Show GEE code</summary>

```javascript
//--------------------------MODIS (unscaled, for reference)---------------------------

var MODIS_Aqua_day = ee.ImageCollection('MODIS/061/MYD11A1')
  .filterDate('2022-01-01', '2022-10-10')
  .filter(ee.Filter.calendarRange(5, 9,'month'))
  .filterBounds(Beijing)
  .select('LST_Day_1km');

print(MODIS_Aqua_day, "MODIS_AQUA");

var MODIS_Terra_day = ee.ImageCollection('MODIS/061/MOD11A1')
  .filterDate('2022-01-01', '2022-10-10')
  .filter(ee.Filter.calendarRange(5, 9,'month'))
  .filterBounds(Beijing)
  .select('LST_Day_1km');

print(MODIS_Terra_day, "MODIS_Terra");

//--------------------------MODIS scaling---------------------------

function MODISscale(image) {
  var temp = image.select('LST_.*').multiply(0.02).subtract(273.1);
  return image.addBands(temp, null, true);
}

var MODIS_Aqua_day = ee.ImageCollection('MODIS/061/MYD11A1')
  .filterDate('2022-01-01', '2022-10-10')
  .filter(ee.Filter.calendarRange(5, 9,'month'))
  .select('LST_Day_1km')
  .map(MODISscale)
  .filterBounds(Beijing);

var MODIS_Terra_day = ee.ImageCollection('MODIS/061/MOD11A1')
  .filterDate('2022-01-01', '2022-10-10')
  .filter(ee.Filter.calendarRange(5, 9,'month'))
  .filterBounds(Beijing)
  .select('LST_Day_1km')
  .map(MODISscale);

//--------------------------MODIS mean image---------------------------

var landSurfaceTemperatureVis = {
  min: 15, max: 45,
  palette: [
    '040274', '040281', '0502a3', '0502b8', '0502ce', '0502e6',
    '0602ff', '235cb1', '307ef3', '269db1', '30c8e2', '32d3ef',
    '3be285', '3ff38f', '86e26f', '3ae237', 'b5e22e', 'd6e21f',
    'fff705', 'ffd611', 'ffb613', 'ff8b13', 'ff6e08', 'ff500d',
    'ff0000', 'de0101', 'c21301', 'a71001', '911003'
  ],
};

var mean_aqua_terra = MODIS_Aqua_day.merge(MODIS_Terra_day)
  .reduce(ee.Reducer.mean())
  .clip(Beijing);

Map.addLayer(mean_aqua_terra, landSurfaceTemperatureVis,
    'MODIS Land Surface Temperature');

//--------------------------Time series---------------------------

var aqua_terra = MODIS_Aqua_day.merge(MODIS_Terra_day);

var timeseries = ui.Chart.image.series({
  imageCollection: aqua_terra,
  region: Beijing,
  reducer: ee.Reducer.mean(),
  scale: 1000,
  xProperty: 'system:time_start'
}).setOptions({
  title: 'Temperature time series',
  vAxis: {title: 'LST Celsius'}
});
print(timeseries);
```

</details>

![Figure 2: MODIS (Aqua + Terra merged) daily mean Land Surface Temperature time series for Beijing, May–September 2022. The 306 observations reveal clear seasonal structure: temperatures peak at approximately **~39°C** in early June, followed by a sharp drop to around **15–16°C** in late June coinciding with the onset of the main monsoon season (though, as discussed in Section 2.6, these extreme lows are heavily influenced by unmasked cloud cover rather than true surface cooling). A secondary warm spell (~37°C) is visible in mid-August before temperatures decline through September to approximately 25–30°C. This intra-seasonal variability would be entirely invisible with Landsat's 16-day revisit.](images/week9_modis_timeseries.png)

![Figure 3: Mean summer LST derived from merged MODIS Aqua and Terra collections (May–September 2022) at **1km resolution** over Beijing municipality. The **central urban core and southern plains** show consistently higher temperatures (yellow–red), while the mountainous northern fringe remains cooler (blue–green) throughout the summer. Comparing this with **Figure 1** (Landsat, 30m), both confirm the same macro-pattern — the urban core and Daxing are the warmest areas, the northern mountain districts the coolest. However, Landsat reveals finer spatial heterogeneity — individual roads, industrial zones, and water bodies are distinguishable — while MODIS smooths these out. This trade-off motivates using Landsat for the spatial **heat index** (Section 2.5) and MODIS for the **temporal trend** (Section 2.6).](images/week9_modis_mean.png)

For the per-district time series, the workshop uses `seriesByRegion` to plot a separate line for each administrative unit. With 11 GADM Level 3 districts and 306 merged Aqua+Terra images, the element count (11 × 306 = 3,366) stays within GEE's 5,000 element limit, allowing the full merged dataset to be used.

<details>
<summary>Show GEE code</summary>

```javascript
//--------------------------Statistics per level 2---------------------------

var gadm3 = ee.FeatureCollection('projects/week6-gee-coursework/assets/gadm41_CHN_3');
var Beijing_level2 = gadm3.filter(ee.Filter.eq('NAME_2', 'Beijing'));
print('Beijing spatial units:', Beijing_level2.size());
print('Unit names:', Beijing_level2.aggregate_array('NAME_3'));

Map.addLayer(Beijing_level2, {}, 'Second Level Administrative Units_2');

print(subtracted_mean);

var mean_Landsat_level2 = subtracted_mean.reduceRegions({
  collection: Beijing_level2,
  reducer: ee.Reducer.mean(),
  scale: 30,
});

Map.addLayer(mean_Landsat_level2, {}, 'mean_Landsat_level2');

Export.table.toDrive({
  collection: mean_Landsat_level2,
  description: 'mean_Landsat_level2',
  fileFormat: 'GeoJSON'
});

//--------------------------MODIS per spatial unit---------------------------

var timeseries_per_unit = ui.Chart.image.seriesByRegion({
  imageCollection: aqua_terra,
  regions: Beijing_level2,
  reducer: ee.Reducer.mean(),
  scale: 1000,
  seriesProperty: 'NAME_3',
  xProperty: 'system:time_start'
}).setOptions({
  title: 'Average temp per spatial unit',
  hAxis: {title: 'Date', titleTextStyle: {italic: false, bold: true}},
  vAxis: {
    title: 'LST Celsius',
    titleTextStyle: {italic: false, bold: true}
  },
  lineWidth: 1
});

print(timeseries_per_unit);

//--------------------------Export triplets CSV---------------------------

var triplets = aqua_terra.map(function(image) {
  return image.select('LST_Day_1km').reduceRegions({
    collection: Beijing_level2.select(['GID_3']),
    reducer: ee.Reducer.mean(),
    scale: 30
  }).filter(ee.Filter.neq('mean', null))
    .map(function(f) {
      return f.set('imageId', image.id());
    });
}).flatten();

print(triplets.first(), "trip");

Export.table.toDrive({
  collection: triplets,
  description: 'triplets_beijing',
  fileNamePrefix: 'triplets_beijing',
  fileFormat: 'CSV'
});
```

</details>

![Figure 4: Daily MODIS LST per district across Beijing (Aqua + Terra merged, May–September 2022), with all 11 GADM Level 3 districts clearly labelled. The lines separate into two visible clusters: a **warmer group** (Daxing, Beijing central urban area, Shunyi, Fangshan) peaking at approximately **40–42°C** in late May and early June, and a **cooler mountain group** (Huairou, Mentougou, Yanqing, Changping, Miyun, Pinggu) running consistently 5–8°C lower throughout the period. All districts register the sharp late-June temperature drop (some falling below 10°C) associated with monsoon **cloud cover** (see Section 2.6 on cloud contamination), and all show partial recovery in August before declining through September. The persistent spread between the two clusters throughout the summer confirms that the **urban–rural thermal gradient** is a structural feature of Beijing's heat environment, not merely an artefact of a single hot day.](images/week9_modis_per_district.png)

::: {.callout-note}
## Why Export Triplets?
The **triplets CSV** — one row per (district, date, mean LST) — is the format needed for time series modelling in R. With 153 Aqua + 153 Terra images per district, there are sufficient observations to run a **Mann-Kendall trend test** and determine whether any district shows a statistically significant warming or cooling trend over the summer period.
:::

### 2.5 Workshop: Heat Index Classification in GEE and R

The final step classifies each district into a **heat hazard level** based on **percentile rank** of mean summer Landsat LST. This replicates the BBC heat vulnerability index methodology. I implemented this in both GEE and R to cross-check results.

One thing I noticed while doing this: the BBC method was originally designed for datasets with hundreds of spatial units (like UK postcodes or wards). Applied to Beijing's 11 districts, the p99 threshold ends up isolating just a single district — which is mathematically fine but feels a bit blunt for only 11 units. A standard deviation-based classification might actually be more appropriate at this scale, though that's beyond the scope of this workshop.

#### GEE Implementation

<details>
<summary>Show GEE code</summary>

```javascript
//--------------------------Heat index (GEE)---------------------------

var percentiles = mean_Landsat_level2.reduceColumns({
  reducer: ee.Reducer.percentile([10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 99, 100]),
  selectors: ['mean']
});

print(percentiles, "percentile");

var p40  = ee.Number(percentiles.get('p40'));
var p70  = ee.Number(percentiles.get('p70'));
var p90  = ee.Number(percentiles.get('p90'));
var p99  = ee.Number(percentiles.get('p99'));
var p100 = ee.Number(percentiles.get('p100'));

print(p40, "p40");

var resub = mean_Landsat_level2.map(function(feat) {
  return ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).lt(p40).and(ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).gt(0)),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 1}),
  ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).gt(p40).and(ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).lt(p70)),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 2}),
  ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).gt(p70).and(ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).lt(p90)),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 3}),
  ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).gt(p90).and(ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).lt(p99)),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 4}),
  ee.Algorithms.If(
    ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).gt(p99).and(ee.Number(feat.get('mean')).lte(p100)),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 5}),
    feat.set({'percentile_cat': 0}))))));
});

var visParams_vec = {
  'palette': ['#fee5d9', '#fcae91', '#fb6a4a', '#de2d26', '#a50f15'],
  'min': 0.0,
  'max': 5,
  'opacity': 0.8,
};

var image = ee.Image().float().paint(resub, 'percentile_cat');

Map.addLayer(image, visParams_vec, 'Percentile_cat');

// Export Landsat LST raster for R interactive map
Export.image.toDrive({
  image: subtracted_mean,
  description: 'subtracted_mean',
  region: Beijing,
  scale: 30,
  fileFormat: 'GeoTIFF'
});
```

</details>

![Figure 5: Heat hazard index by district across Beijing, classified into five **percentile-based levels** in GEE. **Daxing** and the **central urban core (Beijing)** appear at the highest heat levels, while the mountain districts (Huairou, Yanqing, Mentougou) in the north and west occupy the lowest levels (Level 1–2), consistent with the spatial LST patterns observed in **Figures 1 and 3**.](images/week9_heat_index.png)

#### R Implementation

The GeoJSON exported from GEE was loaded into R for independent heat index calculation and visualisation using `tmap`. The **percentile breakpoints** for Beijing's 11 districts are: p40 = 32.04°C, p70 = 34.71°C, p90 = 36.34°C, p99 = 36.55°C — a relatively **narrow range of ~7°C** reflecting the mix of warm urban and cool mountain districts.

<details>
<summary>Show R code</summary>

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(tmap)

# Read GeoJSON exported from GEE
temp <- st_read("E:/Remote/week9/mean_Landsat_level2.geojson")

# Plot mean LST per district
tmap_mode("plot")
tm1 <- tm_shape(temp) + 
  tm_polygons("mean", palette = "Reds") +
  tm_legend(show = TRUE) +
  tm_layout(frame = FALSE)
tm1

# Percentile rank function (from StackExchange)
perc.rank <- function(x) trunc(rank(x))/length(x)

percentile <- temp %>% 
  mutate(percentile1 = (perc.rank(mean)) * 100) %>%
  mutate(Level = case_when(
    between(percentile1, 0,  39.99) ~ "1",
    between(percentile1, 40, 69.99) ~ "2",
    between(percentile1, 70, 89.99) ~ "3",
    between(percentile1, 90, 98.99) ~ "4",
    between(percentile1, 99, 100)   ~ "5"
  ))

# Verify breakpoints
test <- quantile(temp$mean, c(0.39999, 0.69999, 0.8999, 0.98999, 1.0))
print(test)

# Heat index static map
tm2 <- tm_shape(percentile) + 
  tm_polygons("Level", palette = "Reds") +
  tm_legend(show = TRUE) +
  tm_layout(frame = FALSE)
tm2
```

</details>

![Figure 6: Mean summer Landsat LST per district, Beijing (R/tmap). **Daxing** in the southern plain records the highest mean temperature (**36.6°C**), while **Huairou** in the northeast mountains records the lowest (**30.7°C**), a spread of approximately 6°C driven primarily by differences in elevation, land cover, and distance from the urban core.](images/week9_r_lst_mean.png)

![Figure 7: Heat hazard index map produced in R using tmap, reproduced from the GeoJSON exported from GEE. In both the GEE output (**Figure 5**) and this R output, **Daxing** is consistently classified at **Level 5** and the northern mountain districts (Yanqing, Huairou) at **Level 1**, confirming that both implementations produce equivalent classifications. The percentile breakpoints computed in R (p40 = 32.04°C, p70 = 34.71°C, p90 = 36.34°C) confirm the **distribution is narrow** — all 11 districts fall within a ~7°C range — making the **relative ranking** the more policy-relevant metric rather than absolute temperature values.](images/week9_r_heat_map.png)

The interactive map overlays the 30m Landsat LST raster with the district-level heat index polygons, allowing inspection of the correspondence between **pixel-level temperature variation** and the **district-level classification**.

<details>
<summary>Show R code — interactive map</summary>

```r
library(terra)

temp_rast <- rast("E:/Remote/week9/subtracted_mean.tif")

# Downsample to reduce file size for rendering
temp_rast_small <- aggregate(temp_rast, fact = 4)

tmap_mode("view")

interactive <- tm_shape(temp_rast_small) +
  tm_raster(col = "ST_B10_mean",
            col.scale = tm_scale_continuous(values = "brewer.reds")) + 
  tm_shape(percentile) + 
  tm_polygons(fill = "Level",
              fill.scale = tm_scale_categorical(values = "brewer.reds"),
              fill_alpha = 0.7) +
  tm_legend()

interactive
```

</details>

**Figure 8:** Interactive tmap view overlaying the Landsat LST raster (120m resampled) and the heat hazard district polygons at 70% opacity. This allows inspection of the correspondence between pixel-level temperature and the district-level classification.

```{=html}
<iframe src="images/week9.html" 
        width="100%" 
        height="500px" 
        frameborder="0">
</iframe>
```

### 2.6 Extended Analysis: Mann-Kendall Trend Test

Going beyond the workshop, I ran a **Mann-Kendall trend test** on the MODIS triplets CSV to determine whether each district showed a **statistically significant temperature trend** over the May–September 2022 period.

<details>
<summary>Show R code</summary>

```r
library(tidyverse)
library(Kendall)

triplets <- read_csv("E:/Remote/week9/triplets_beijing.csv")

# Mann-Kendall per district
results <- triplets %>%
  group_by(GID_3) %>%
  summarise(
    mk_tau = MannKendall(mean)$tau,
    mk_pval = MannKendall(mean)$sl
  )

# Join district names
name_lookup <- percentile %>% 
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  select(GID_3, NAME_3)

results_named <- results %>%
  left_join(name_lookup, by = "GID_3") %>%
  select(NAME_3, GID_3, mk_tau, mk_pval)

print(results_named)

# LST distribution by district
triplets_named <- triplets %>%
  left_join(name_lookup, by = "GID_3")

ggplot(triplets_named, aes(x = NAME_3, y = mean, fill = NAME_3)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(x = "District", y = "LST (°C)", 
       title = "LST Distribution by Beijing District") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        legend.position = "none")
```

</details>

All 11 districts show a **statistically significant negative tau** (all p < 0.001), indicating a consistent **cooling trend** across the summer period. This reflects the seasonal influence of the **East Asian summer monsoon**: temperatures peak in May–June then decline through July–August as monsoon rainfall increases cloud cover and reduces incoming solar radiation. **Daxing** shows the steepest decline (τ = −0.349), consistent with its low-lying plain location most exposed to monsoonal rainfall, while **Yanqing** shows the weakest trend (τ = −0.145), reflecting its more sheltered mountainous position.

That said, I should note that the monsoon season also brings heavy cloud cover, and the workshop didn't apply any **QA bitmask cloud filtering** to the MODIS data. This means some of those very sharp dips in the time series — particularly the drop to around 7°C visible in early June (Figure 2) — are almost certainly **cloud contamination** rather than real surface cooling. MODIS is an optical sensor and picks up cloud-top temperatures when thick cloud is present. A more rigorous approach would apply the MODIS QA band to mask cloudy pixels before computing the time series. I followed the workshop method here, which skips that step to keep the workflow manageable, but it's worth keeping in mind when interpreting the Mann-Kendall results — the negative tau values might partly reflect increasing cloud cover through the monsoon season rather than pure surface cooling.

![Figure 9: Boxplot of daily MODIS LST distribution by Beijing district (May–September 2022). **Beijing** (central urban area) shows a high median (~32°C) with a compact IQR, reflecting consistent heat retention by dense impervious surfaces. **Daxing** records the most extreme high-temperature outliers (approaching ~45°C on individual days), consistent with its flat, low-albedo agricultural plains being particularly susceptible to intense daytime heating. The mountain districts — **Huairou**, **Mentougou**, and **Yanqing** — cluster at the lower end (~26–28°C median), confirming the persistent urban–rural thermal gradient observed in the spatial maps (**Figures 1 and 3**). **Shunyi** and **Fangshan** occupy a middle-to-warm position, reflecting their mixed peri-urban character.](images/week9_r_boxplot.png)

### 2.7 Literature: Heat Justice and EO Evidence

[@wilson2020] and [@li2022] both argue that **heat maps only become policy-relevant when overlaid with socio-economic data** — knowing *where* it is hot is less useful than knowing *who* is exposed there and *why*. [@maclachlan2021] provide a direct example in practice: using Landsat LST alongside green space and population density data to identify **priority intervention zones** in Perth, Australia. For Beijing, the heat index produced here (**Figures 5 and 7**) and the LST distribution (**Figure 9**) together show that **Daxing** and the **central urban core** consistently record the highest temperatures. Whether those areas also have lower green cover and higher population vulnerability is a question the analysis cannot yet answer, but it is exactly the kind of **equity question** that [@gee_book2023] points towards.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Connecting Heat Index to Urban Policy
The **Level 4–5 districts** identified here — primarily **Daxing** and the **central urban core** — could form the basis for targeted urban greening or heat emergency planning. Overlaying this heat index with **population density**, elderly population share, and green cover data would transform a thermal map into a genuine **vulnerability assessment**.
:::

---

## 3. Personal Reflection

### 3.1 From Classification to Temperature

Weeks 7 and 8 produced land cover maps of Beijing — urban core, vegetation, bare earth, water. This week asks what those surfaces actually feel like in summer. The connection is immediate and satisfying: the areas that classified as **dense urban or bare earth** in weeks 7 and 8 are the same areas that appear yellow–green on the Landsat LST map (**Figure 1**). The heat index confirms this quantitatively, placing **Daxing** and the **central urban core** at the top of the thermal distribution — Daxing because of its flat, low-albedo agricultural plains, and the urban core because of the sheer density of impervious surface and building stock.

What surprised me most was the **seasonal structure** in the MODIS time series — and the lesson it taught me about working with raw satellite data. Looking at Figure 2, I first noticed that temperatures peaked in late May and early June rather than July or August as I expected. Then came the dramatic late-June drop, with some districts falling below 10°C. My initial reaction was to interpret this as a powerful seasonal cooling signal driven by the East Asian monsoon rain belt arriving at Beijing's latitude — the **Mann-Kendall analysis** even seemed to confirm this, showing a statistically significant **negative tau** across all 11 districts.

But then I looked more carefully at the physics. A daytime land surface temperature of 7–10°C in Beijing in June is physically implausible — even with heavy rainfall, the ground doesn't cool to near-freezing in summer. What's actually happening is that the workshop methodology skips **QA bitmask cloud filtering**, so when thick monsoon cloud cover rolls in, the optical sensor picks up freezing **cloud-top temperatures** and averages them straight into the time series alongside real surface readings. The "dramatic cooling" in the chart is largely a data artefact rather than a real surface phenomenon. It was a useful reminder that a Mann-Kendall test can return a highly significant result and still be telling the wrong physical story if the data preprocessing was incomplete upstream.

### 3.2 Limitations and the Question of What "Hot" Means

The heat index classification is **relative**, not absolute. A Level 5 district in Beijing is the hottest relative to other districts in the municipality, but that says nothing about absolute exposure or who lives in the hottest zones. [@klinenberg1999]'s Chicago analysis is a reminder that the deadliest heat events occur where heat coincides with **social vulnerability**: elderly people living alone, poorly ventilated housing, and neighbourhoods without accessible cool public spaces. The heat index tells you **where** it is hottest; it does not tell you **who** bears the greatest burden.

The LST data also captures **surface temperature**, not **air temperature**. Rooftops and roads drive very high LST values, but the temperature experienced by a person standing on the street may be substantially lower — especially if tree canopy is present. [@wilson2020] identifies **tree cover** as one of the strongest mediators of this gap, and [@nowak2022] show its systematic absence in historically underinvested areas.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Surface Temperature Is Not Air Temperature
**LST** from Landsat or MODIS measures the temperature of the **surface material** — not the air temperature that people experience. In dense urban areas with dark impervious surfaces, LST can exceed air temperature by **10–15°C**. This distinction matters when translating satellite data into health risk estimates [@maclachlan2021].
:::

There are also two places where the workshop methodology is deliberately simplified in ways that are worth being honest about. The cloud contamination issue already covered in Section 2.6 is one — skipping QA bitmasking keeps the workflow clean for learning purposes, but it does mean the time series statistics are noisier than they should be. The other is the **sample size for the percentile heat index**: the BBC method this is based on really comes into its own with hundreds of spatial units like UK wards or US census tracts. With only 11 Beijing districts, the p99 cutoff essentially just isolates the single hottest district, which feels a bit mechanical. The classification would probably be more informative applied at a finer spatial scale — sub-district or grid cell level — where the distribution has more shape to it. Neither of these undermines what I produced here, but they're the first things I'd address if taking this further.

---

## 4. References

::: {#refs}
:::
